Generates an ECDSA-P256 self-signed X.509 certificate valid for the

### fn fn h3_make_self_signed_cert(cert_path: string, key_path: string, cn: string, days: i32) -> !i32
Generates an ECDSA-P256 self-signed X.509 certificate valid for the
next `days` days and writes it as PEM to `cert_path` and `key_path`.
The SAN extension covers `DNS:localhost` and `IP:127.0.0.1`, so it's
usable as-is for local dev servers and loopback tests.

```glide
let r: !i32 = h3_make_self_signed_cert("cert.pem", "key.pem", "localhost", 365);
if !r.ok { return 1; }
http3_listen(4443, "cert.pem", "key.pem", my_handler);
result: ok(0) on success
```

### fn fn http3_listen(port: i32, cert_path: string, key_path: string, handler: fn(*HttpRequest) -> HttpResponse) -> !i32
Run an HTTP/3 server on `port` (UDP), accepting one connection at a
time. Each accepted client gets its full request dispatched to
`handler`; the returned `HttpResponse` is sent back, then the
connection is torn down.

`cert_path` + `key_path` are PEM files (same layout as `https_listen`).

```glide
fn root(req: *HttpRequest) -> HttpResponse {
    return HttpResponse::ok().body("hello over QUIC");
}

fn main() -> i32 {
    http3_listen(443, "cert.pem", "key.pem", root);
    return 0;
}
```

Concurrency: a single C reader thread owns the UDP socket and demuxes
inbound packets across up to 64 simultaneous connections, advancing
each via ngtcp2 + nghttp3. Each accepted connection is dispatched to
`handler` on its own pthread, so slow handlers don't block other
clients' handshake completion or accept dispatch. Stream-level
concurrency within a connection (HTTP/3 PUSH, streaming response
bodies, 0-RTT) is not supported in v1.

### fn fn http3_get(url: string, tls_insecure: bool) -> !*HttpResponse
Single-shot HTTP/3 GET. Opens a QUIC connection, performs the TLS 1.3
+ QUIC handshake (ALPN `h3`), sends one request stream and reads the
response back. Returns the response (status, headers, body) wrapped
in the existing `HttpResponse` shape.

`tls_insecure = true` disables certificate verification — local dev
against self-signed certs only.

```glide
let r: !*HttpResponse = http3_get("https://example.com/", false);
if r.ok { println!(r.val.status); println!(r.val.body); }
```

### fn fn http3_request(method: string, url: string, extra_headers: string, body: string, tls_insecure: bool) -> !*HttpResponse
Generic single-shot HTTP/3 request. `method` is `"GET"`, `"POST"`,
`"PUT"`, etc; `extra_headers` is a CRLF-joined `Name: Value` block
(same shape as `HttpClientRequest.headers`); `body` is the request
payload.

```glide
let r: !*HttpResponse = http3_request(
    "POST", "https://api.example.com/x", "Content-Type: application/json\r\n",
    "{\"hi\":1}", false);
```

### struct struct H3SessionCache
Caches TLS session tickets + ngtcp2 0-RTT transport params so repeat
requests to the same host can skip a full handshake. Pass a single
`H3SessionCache` across multiple `http3_request_cached` calls and the
second + later connections to the same `(host, port)` will resume
the prior TLS session and send the request as 0-RTT (saves ~1 RTT).

In-memory only in v1 — the cache lives as long as the
`H3SessionCache` does. Call `free()` to release.

```glide
let c: *H3SessionCache = H3SessionCache::new();
defer c.free();
let r1: !*HttpResponse = http3_request_cached("GET", url, "", "", false, c);
r1 went through a full handshake; ticket cached.
let r2: !*HttpResponse = http3_request_cached("GET", url, "", "", false, c);
r2 resumed the prior session.
```

### fn fn http3_request_cached(method: string, url: string, extra_headers: string, body: string, tls_insecure: bool, cache: *H3SessionCache) -> !*HttpResponse
Same shape as `http3_request` but threads a `H3SessionCache` for
TLS session resumption + 0-RTT early data on repeat calls to the
same host. Safe to call concurrently from multiple threads against
the same cache (internal mutex serialises store/lookup).

### struct struct ParsedH3Url

## stdlib::http::handler
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/http/handler.glide
package: stdlib

Shared wrap logic used by both `@handler` and `@get/@post/etc`. Emits

### fn fn _emit_handler_wrapper(s: *Stmt, wrapper_name: string, renamed_name: string) -> *Vector<Stmt>
Shared wrap logic used by both `@handler` and `@get/@post/etc`. Emits
the user fn renamed to `renamed_name` (preserving the original typed
body) plus a `fn(*HttpRequest) -> HttpResponse` wrapper at
`wrapper_name` that calls it with each param extracted from the
request. Param dispatch table is documented above.

## stdlib::http::hpack
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/http/hpack.glide
package: stdlib

Output of decode_block: a list of (name, value) pairs in wire order.

### struct struct HpackEncoder

### struct struct HpackDecoder

### struct struct HpackHeader
Output of decode_block: a list of (name, value) pairs in wire order.
Pseudo-headers (`:method`, `:status`, etc.) are surfaced as-is —
users sort response vs request semantics.

## stdlib::http::jwt
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/http/jwt.glide
package: stdlib

### struct struct JwtClaims

### fn fn jwt_verify(token: string, key: string) -> !JwtClaims

## stdlib::http::multipart
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/http/multipart.glide
package: stdlib

### struct struct Multipart

### struct struct UploadedFile

### struct struct MultipartForm

## stdlib::http::proxy
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/http/proxy.glide
package: stdlib

Reverse-proxy configuration. The minimal setup is an upstream

### struct struct ReverseProxyConfig
Reverse-proxy configuration. The minimal setup is an upstream
origin such as `http://127.0.0.1:9000`; the proxy forwards the
incoming method, path, body and safe end-to-end headers.

`strip_prefix` is useful for mounting an app under a local prefix:
with `strip_prefix = "/api"`, `/api/users?x=1` is sent upstream as
`/users?x=1`.

### fn fn reverse_proxy(cfg: *ReverseProxyConfig, req: *HttpRequest) -> HttpResponse
Forward `req` to `cfg.upstream` and lower the upstream response
back into the server-side `HttpResponse` shape. Network and parse
errors become `502 Bad Gateway`.

### fn fn proxy_build_upstream_request(cfg: *ReverseProxyConfig, req: *HttpRequest) -> *HttpClientRequest
Build the client request that `reverse_proxy` will send upstream.
Exposed for tests and for custom proxy pipelines that want to
inspect or adjust the request before calling `HttpClient::do`.

### fn fn proxy_target_url(cfg: *ReverseProxyConfig, path: string) -> string
Compute the upstream URL for a request path.

### fn fn proxy_filter_request_headers(block: string, preserve_host: bool) -> string
Drop hop-by-hop headers from the request before proxying. `Host`
is also dropped unless `preserve_host` is true, because the client
transport will stamp the upstream host by default.

### fn fn proxy_filter_response_headers(block: string) -> string
Drop hop-by-hop and framing headers from an upstream response. The
local server re-emits `Content-Length` and `Connection` itself.

### fn fn proxy_rewrite_location(loc: string, upstream: string, public_base: string) -> string
Rewrite an absolute upstream `Location` value to the public proxy
base. Relative locations are left untouched.

## stdlib::http::route
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/http/route.glide
package: stdlib

`/`, `/foo`, `/foo/:id`, `/static/*rest` — basic shape check. Returns

### fn fn _validate_path(path: string) -> string
`/`, `/foo`, `/foo/:id`, `/static/*rest` — basic shape check. Returns
"" when ok, otherwise a single-line error describing the first
offence so the diagnostic stays scannable.

### fn fn _validate_path_params(path: string, params: *Vector<Param>) -> string
For each `:name` segment in `path`, look for a matching `name: Path<T>`
in `params`. Wildcards `*name` map to `name: Path<string>`. Mismatch
surfaces as a compile-time diagnostic so a typo doesn't degrade to
a runtime 400/404.

Handlers that take a single `req: *HttpRequest` are opting out of the
typed-extraction sugar — they'll read params via `req.param("id")`
themselves. Skip the check entirely in that shape.

### fn fn _is_route_attr_name(n: string) -> bool

## stdlib::http::router
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/http/router.glide
package: stdlib

One piece of a compiled route pattern.

### const const RS_LITERAL: i32

### const const RS_PARAM: i32

### const const RS_WILDCARD: i32

### struct struct RouteSegment
One piece of a compiled route pattern.

`kind` is `RS_LITERAL` (the segment text must match), `RS_PARAM`
(any one segment, captured under `text`), or `RS_WILDCARD` (the
rest of the path, captured under `text`). Wildcard segments can
only appear last; later segments after a `*` are ignored.

### struct struct Route
One row of the routing table.

`local_mws` is the per-route middleware chain — populated when the
route was mounted via `Router::scope(prefix, sub)`. Parent
middleware runs first, then `local_mws` (in registration order),
then the handler.

### const const ANY_METHOD: string
Wildcard sentinel for `Router::any`. `dispatch` matches any verb when
`route.method` equals this exact string.

### struct struct Router
HTTP method router. Stores routes in declaration order; the first
match wins. Middleware in `mw` runs before the matched handler in
registration order. A separate "not found" handler covers
no-match dispatches; defaults to a plain 404 with body
`"not found"`.

### struct struct Chain
Per-dispatch chain context. Threaded through every middleware so
each can call `chain_next(c)` to advance to the next middleware
(or the route handler when the middleware list is exhausted).
Middleware that returns without calling `chain_next` short-
circuits the chain — the rest of the middleware AND the route
handler are skipped.

### fn fn chain_next(c: *Chain) -> HttpResponse
Advance the middleware chain. The first call from inside
middleware N runs middleware N+1; once the list is exhausted the
route handler runs.

```glide
fn logger(req: *HttpRequest, c: *Chain) -> HttpResponse {
    println!("[", req.method, "] ", req.path);
    let resp: HttpResponse = chain_next(c);
    println!("  -> ", resp.status);
    return resp;
}
```

## stdlib::http::sse
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/http/sse.glide
package: stdlib

One Server-Sent Event. Fields map directly to wire lines:

### struct struct SseEvent
One Server-Sent Event. Fields map directly to wire lines:
  `event:` (optional name), `id:` (optional ID for Last-Event-ID),
  `data:` (one or many lines), `retry:` (ms before client retry, 0 = omit).

### fn fn sse_format(e: SseEvent) -> string
Serialize an event to wire bytes. Multi-line data emits one
`data: ...` per line (per spec; the client joins them on `\n`).
Empty event = single blank line (a keepalive frame).

```glide
sse_format(SseEvent::named("tick", "1"));
-> "event: tick\ndata: 1\n\n"
```

### fn fn sse_comment(text: string) -> string
SSE comment line — useful as a heartbeat / keepalive so the
connection doesn't time out on intermediaries (nginx default
proxy_read_timeout = 60s). Pair with a timer to emit every ~15-30s.

```glide
": heartbeat\n\n"
sse_comment("heartbeat");
```

### fn fn sse_keepalive() -> string
Standard heartbeat frame — just a colon + double newline, no
payload. Smaller than `sse_comment("ping")` on the wire.

### fn fn sse_last_event_id(req: *HttpRequest) -> string
Pull the client's `Last-Event-ID` header. When the browser
reconnects after a transient drop it sends back the last `id:`
it saw. Empty string when the header is absent.

### fn fn sse_response(source: *dyn ChunkSource) -> HttpResponse
Wrap `source` in a streaming response with the right
Content-Type and cache-busting headers for SSE.

Headers set:
  * `Content-Type: text/event-stream`
  * `Cache-Control: no-cache`
  * `X-Accel-Buffering: no` — disables buffering on nginx proxies
  * `Connection: keep-alive`

### const const SSE_END: string
Fan-out source over a chan. Each event sent into the chan becomes
a frame on the client. To END the stream, send a sentinel event
where `event.event` equals `SSE_END` — the source treats that as
EOF and signals chunked transfer to close.

```glide
let events: chan<SseEvent> = make_chan(64);
fn stream(_req: *HttpRequest) -> HttpResponse {
    let src: *SseChannel = SseChannel::new(&events);
    return sse_response(src as *dyn ChunkSource);
}
elsewhere: events.send(SseEvent::named("ping", "1"));
close: events.send(SseEvent { event: SSE_END, ... });
```

### struct struct SseChannel

## stdlib::http::static
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/http/static.glide
package: stdlib

Configuration for a static-file mount.

### struct struct StaticOpts
Configuration for a static-file mount.

`root` is the directory on disk; `url_prefix` is the URL path
segment under which files become reachable (e.g. `"/static"` means
`GET /static/css/site.css` reads `<root>/css/site.css`).

`cache_control` is emitted as the `Cache-Control` response header
when non-empty — pass `"public, max-age=31536000, immutable"` for
fingerprinted asset bundles, or `"no-cache"` to force revalidation.

### fn fn serve_dir(r: *Router, opts: StaticOpts)
Mount a directory on the router. Subsequent `GET /<url_prefix>/...`
requests serve files from `<root>/...`. The current implementation
supports a single mount per router (the root + cache settings live
in process-wide statics); call `serve_dir` once at startup.

```glide
serve_dir(r, StaticOpts {
    root:          "./public",
    url_prefix:    "/assets",
    cache_control: "public, max-age=86400",
});
```

## stdlib::http::typed
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/http/typed.glide
package: stdlib

Generic wrapper around a value that should be serialised as the

### struct struct Json
Generic wrapper around a value that should be serialised as the
JSON body of an HTTP response. The struct is intentionally a
single-field carrier — the value gets to `into_response()` via
the `IntoResponse` impl below, which calls `T.to_json()` (so `T`
must implement `JsonBind`).

```glide
Build:
let r: HttpResponse = json_respond(StructLegal { nome: "alice", idade: some(30) });
```

### fn fn json_respond(v: T) -> HttpResponse
One-step from typed value to HttpResponse via the `IntoResponse`
trait. Equivalent to `Json::wrap(v).into_response()` but spelt as
a free fn so the chained dispatch monomorphizes cleanly across
the call boundary.

```glide
fn create(req: *HttpRequest) -> HttpResponse {
    let user: StructLegal = /* ... */;
    return json_respond(user);
}
```

### trait trait IntoResponse
Convert any "response-shaped" value into a final HttpResponse the
server loop can write to the wire. Implemented for HttpResponse
itself (identity) and for `Json<T>` when T binds to JSON. Future
implementors might cover `string` (text reply), `?T`, error types,
etc.; nothing in stdlib::http::router calls `into_response`
today, so impls are purely for handler-tail ergonomics.

## stdlib::mail::imap
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/mail/imap.glide
package: stdlib

### struct struct ImapSession

### struct struct ImapResponse

## stdlib::mail::message
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/mail/message.glide
package: stdlib

Parse an RFC 5322 message into a Mail. Splits header block from body

### struct struct Attachment

### struct struct Mail

### fn fn parse_mail(raw: string) -> !*Mail
Parse an RFC 5322 message into a Mail. Splits header block from body
and decodes the headline fields. MIME multipart bodies are kept as
the raw body string — full MIME parsing is deferred (good enough
for round-trip metadata access in v1).

```glide
let raw: string = pop3.retr(1).val;
let r: !*Mail = parse_mail(raw);
if r.ok {
    println!("from:", r.val.from);
    println!("subject:", r.val.subject);
}
```

## stdlib::net::dns
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/net/dns.glide
package: stdlib

Resolve `host` to a list of IP addresses via the system resolver.

### fn fn resolve(host: string) -> !*Vector<*IpAddr>
Resolve `host` to a list of IP addresses via the system resolver.
Returns every A and AAAA record the resolver returns, in the order
it returned them.

```glide
let r: !*Vector<*IpAddr> = resolve("example.com");
if r.ok {
    for let i: i32 = 0; i < r.val.len(); i++ {
        println!(r.val.get(i).to_string());
    }
}
```

### fn fn resolve_v4(host: string) -> !*Vector<*IpAddr>
Same as `resolve` but filters to v4 addresses only.

```glide
let r: !*Vector<*IpAddr> = resolve_v4("example.com");
if r.ok && r.val.len() > 0 {
    println!("first v4:", r.val.get(0).to_string());
}
```

### fn fn resolve_v6(host: string) -> !*Vector<*IpAddr>
Same as `resolve` but filters to v6 addresses only.

```glide
let r: !*Vector<*IpAddr> = resolve_v6("ipv6.google.com");
if r.ok && r.val.len() > 0 {
    println!("first v6:", r.val.get(0).to_string());
}
```

## stdlib::net::ip
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/net/ip.glide
package: stdlib

A v4 or v6 address. `kind` is 4 or 6; the other fields are

### fn fn _bytes_to_string(p: *u8, n: i32) -> string

### struct struct IpAddr
A v4 or v6 address. `kind` is 4 or 6; the other fields are
meaningful only for the matching kind.

V4 layout: `v4` holds the 32-bit address in host-order, e.g.
`127.0.0.1` is `0x7F000001`.

V6 layout: `v6_hi` and `v6_lo` are the upper and lower 64 bits in
host-order. The address `::1` has `v6_hi = 0`, `v6_lo = 1`.

### struct struct SocketAddr

## stdlib::net::listener
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/net/listener.glide
package: stdlib

### struct struct Listener

### struct struct Conn

package: stdlib

### struct struct TcpStream

## stdlib::net::tls
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/net/tls.glide
package: stdlib

Configuration for an outbound TLS connection.

### struct struct TlsConfig
Configuration for an outbound TLS connection.

### struct struct TlsStream

### struct struct TlsListener
Server-side listener: holds the SSL_CTX with cert+key plus the
underlying TCP listening socket. `accept` blocks for the next
client and returns a TlsStream after the handshake succeeds.

### fn fn tls_attach_server(conn_fd: i32, cert_path: string, key_path: string) -> !*TlsStream
Server-side counterpart to `TlsStream::attach`: wrap an already-
accepted plain `Conn` in TLS for protocols that promote inline
(STARTTLS, FTPS-AUTH-TLS, IMAP STARTTLS, POP3 STLS).

On success the returned `TlsStream` owns the underlying socket;
the caller MUST NOT call `conn.close()` afterwards.

```glide
In an SMTP server, after the client sends STARTTLS:
let r: !*TlsStream = tls_attach_server(conn.fd, "cert.pem", "key.pem");
if r.ok { defer r.val.close(); /* communicate over TLS */ }
```

## stdlib::net::udp
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/net/udp.glide
package: stdlib

One received datagram: payload + sender address.

### struct struct UdpSocket

### struct struct UdpRecv
One received datagram: payload + sender address.

## stdlib::net::ws
source: C:\Users\bye45\Desktop\desktop\works\javascomp/src/stdlib/net/ws.glide
package: stdlib

Frame opcodes (RFC 6455 §11.8). Compare against `WsMessage.kind`.

### struct struct WsOp
Frame opcodes (RFC 6455 §11.8). Compare against `WsMessage.kind`.

```glide
match m.kind {
    k if k == WsOp::text()  => println!("text:", m.text),
    k if k == WsOp::ping()  => ws.pong(m.text),
    k if k == WsOp::close() => break,
    _ => {},
}
```

### struct struct WsMessage
One inbound message. `kind` is one of `WsOp::text() / binary() /
close() / ping() / pong()`. `text` carries text-frame payload as
a string; `data` carries binary payload (currently in the same
`string` field — we don't have a dedicated bytes type yet).

### struct struct WebSocket

### struct struct WsParsedUrl
